description: To support dissertation research costs of students in accredited research doctoral programs in the United States (including Puerto Rico and other U.S. territories or possessions). Dissertation awards are not renewable.

grant_sections.definition: Required only for Resubmission or Revision applications, detailing changes made in response to previous critiques.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: This section is mandatory only for Resubmission (A1) or Revision applications. Begin with a concise summary of the major changes made in response to the previous scientific review group (SRG) critique. Follow with a respectful, point-by-point response to all critiques (including scored and unscored comments). Clearly delineate changes made in the application text (e.g., using bolding, italics, or change bars) and provide a strong rationale for any critiques that were not addressed.
guidelines: Only include this section if the application is a Resubmission or Revision type.
Address the summary of the previous scientific review group critique.
Clearly delineate changes made to the application in response to the critiques.
If no changes were made, provide a rationale for not making changes.
search_queries: NIH A1 resubmission introduction requirements
Responding to NIH Summary Statement critiques
Formatting changes in NIH revision application
Rationale for not addressing specific reviewer comments
Tone and style for NIH introduction to resubmission
grant_sections.title: Introduction to Application (Resubmission/Revision)

grant_sections.definition: The detailed description of the research, organized into Significance, Innovation, and Approach.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Structure the narrative strictly into three subsections: Significance, Innovation, and Approach. Significance must establish the scientific premise and explain the importance of the problem. Innovation must describe how the project challenges current paradigms. Approach must detail the methodology for each Specific Aim, including controls, sample sizes, and statistical methods. Explicitly address rigor and reproducibility. Crucially, dedicate a subsection within Approach to potential problems, limitations, and robust alternative strategies for every major experimental step. Adhere strictly to the FOA's page limits.
guidelines: Must be organized into three distinct sections: Significance, Innovation, and Approach.
The Approach section must detail the methodology, potential problems, and alternative strategies.
Clearly articulate the scientific premise for the proposed research.
Adhere strictly to the page limits specified in the funding opportunity announcement (FOA).
search_queries: NIH Research Strategy Significance Innovation Approach structure
Addressing rigor and reproducibility in NIH Approach section
Preliminary data requirements for R01 grant
Detailed experimental design and statistical analysis plan
Mitigating potential pitfalls and alternative strategies NIH
Scientific premise definition and inclusion in research strategy
Page limits for NIH Research Strategy section
Justification of statistical power and sample size
Validation of key biological and chemical resources
grant_sections.title: Research Strategy

grant_sections.definition: A required plan detailing the roles, responsibilities, and administrative structure when multiple Principal Investigators (PD/PIs) are involved.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: If the application involves multiple PD/PIs, provide a detailed plan. Justify the necessity of the multi-PI approach over a single PI model. Clearly define the roles, responsibilities, and specific expertise contribution of each PD/PI relative to the Specific Aims. Outline the administrative structure, including the process for decision-making, budget allocation, and resolving scientific or administrative conflicts among the leadership team. Ensure the plan demonstrates seamless coordination.
guidelines: Only include this section if the application involves multiple PD/PIs.
Describe the rationale for choosing a multiple PD/PI approach.
Clearly define the roles, responsibilities, and administrative structure for each PD/PI.
Outline the process for decision-making, conflict resolution, and communication among the leadership team.
search_queries: NIH Multiple PD/PI leadership plan requirements
Administrative structure for multi-PI grant
Conflict resolution process in team science grants
Defining roles and responsibilities for co-PIs NIH
Rationale for choosing multiple PD/PI approach
grant_sections.title: Multiple PD/PI Leadership Plan

grant_sections.definition: Required section detailing the involvement, care, and procedures related to vertebrate animals in the proposed research.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Address the five required points: 1) Detailed procedures involving animals, 2) Justification for species, strains, ages, sexes, and numbers used, 3) Adequacy of veterinary care, 4) Methods for minimizing pain and distress (including use of tranquilizers, analgesics, and anesthetics), and 5) Method of euthanasia. Ensure the description aligns with institutional IACUC standards and the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals.
guidelines: Provide a description of the proposed procedures involving vertebrate animals.
Justify the use of animals, the species, strains, ages, sexes, and numbers to be used.
Address methods for minimizing pain and distress, including the use of tranquilizers, analgesics, and anesthetics.
Describe the veterinary care and housing facilities.
search_queries: NIH Vertebrate Animals Section five required points
Justification for animal species and numbers in grant
Methods for minimizing pain and distress in research animals
IACUC protocol alignment NIH grant writing
Description of veterinary care and housing facilities NIH
grant_sections.title: Vertebrate Animals

grant_sections.definition: Required section if the research involves the use of select agents or toxins regulated by the CDC or USDA.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: If the research involves select agents or toxins, clearly list the specific agents. Detail the institutional registration status with the CDC or USDA Federal Select Agent Program (FSAP). Describe the security measures, containment facilities (e.g., BSL-3), and handling protocols used to prevent unauthorized access, theft, or release. Confirm oversight by the Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBC).
guidelines: Only include if the research involves the use of select agents or toxins.
Provide a description of the select agents to be used.
Detail the registration status of the entity with the CDC or USDA.
Describe the procedures for securing and handling the select agents.
search_queries: NIH Select Agents and Toxins research requirements
CDC/USDA Select Agent Program registration status
Biosafety procedures for handling select toxins
Institutional Biosafety Committee oversight select agents
Security measures for select agents research
grant_sections.title: Select Agent Research

grant_sections.definition: A plan outlining how scientific data will be managed, preserved, and shared during and after the research project.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Develop a comprehensive Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Plan compliant with the NIH 2023 Policy. Describe the scientific data to be managed and shared (data types, formats, volume). Specify the metadata and standards applied (e.g., FAIR principles). Name the chosen repository (e.g., GEO, dbGaP) and define the preservation and access timelines (no later than publication). Detail the roles, responsibilities, and oversight mechanisms for implementing the plan, including any costs associated with data sharing.
guidelines: Describe the scientific data to be managed and shared, including data types and formats.
Specify the standards to be applied to the scientific data.
Outline the preservation, access, and associated timelines for data sharing.
Detail the roles, responsibilities, and oversight for DMS plan implementation.
search_queries: NIH 2023 Data Management and Sharing Policy requirements
Selecting appropriate NIH data repositories
Metadata standards for scientific data sharing plan
Budgeting for data management and sharing costs
Timeline for data access and preservation NIH DMS
Roles and responsibilities for DMS plan implementation
FAIR principles application in NIH grant writing
Data curation and quality control plan
grant_sections.title: Data Management and Sharing Plan
topics: Oversight and Monitoring of DMS Plan

grant_sections.definition: Required plans addressing the sharing of research resources (e.g., unique reagents, model organisms) developed during the project.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Describe all unique research resources (e.g., novel antibodies, cell lines, animal models) developed during the project that will be shared. Detail the mechanism for sharing (e.g., public repository, MTA). If genomic data is generated, include a separate Genomic Data Sharing (GDS) Plan addressing data submission to NIH repositories (e.g., dbGaP) and consent requirements. If sharing of any resource is restricted, provide a detailed, compelling justification.
guidelines: Address the sharing of research resources such as unique reagents, cell lines, or model organisms.
If applicable, include a Genomic Data Sharing (GDS) Plan.
Explain how the resources will be made available to the scientific community.
If sharing is restricted, provide a detailed justification for the limitations.
search_queries: NIH Research Resource Sharing Plan requirements
Genomic Data Sharing (GDS) Plan components NIH
Sharing unique reagents and model organisms policy
Material Transfer Agreement (MTA) for resource sharing
Justification for restricting research resource availability
Timeline for making research resources available
NIH policy on sharing cell lines and plasmids
grant_sections.title: Resource Sharing Plan(s)
topics: Types of Resources to be Shared

grant_sections.definition: A dedicated form (PHS Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information, R.500) used to provide detailed information on the involvement of human subjects and/or clinical trials.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Complete the PHS Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information form (R.500). Provide detailed narrative attachments covering the study population, recruitment strategies, and inclusion/exclusion criteria. Describe the protections of human subjects from research risks, including the informed consent process and IRB status. If a clinical trial is proposed, include the required protocol synopsis, primary/secondary outcome measures, and a robust Data and Safety Monitoring Plan (DSMP). Ensure compliance with NIH policies regarding the inclusion of women, minorities, and children.
guidelines: Complete the PHS Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information form (R.500).
Provide detailed information regarding human subjects involvement, even if exempt.
If a clinical trial is proposed, include the required protocol synopsis and outcome measures.
Describe the protections of human subjects from research risks.
search_queries: PHS Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information R.500 completion guide

IRB approval and informed consent process documentation
Risk/benefit assessment for human subjects research
Defining primary and secondary outcome measures for NIH trials
Recruitment and retention strategies for clinical trials
grant_sections.title: Human Subjects and Clinical Trials Information
topics: Inclusion of Women, Minorities, and Children

grant_sections.definition: A narrative justification explaining and defending all requested costs listed in the budget forms.
grant_sections.generation_instructions: Provide a detailed narrative justification for all requested costs. For personnel, list each individual (or position), their role, and the exact effort level in person months (calendar, academic, or summer). Justify the necessity of their effort relative to the Specific Aims. For non-personnel costs (supplies, travel, equipment), provide itemized explanations, ensuring alignment with the R&R Budget or Modular Budget forms. If a modular budget is used, provide detailed justification for personnel and consortium costs separately.
guidelines: Provide detailed justification for all requested personnel, including roles and effort levels (person months).
Justify all major cost categories (e.g., equipment, travel, supplies).
If using a detailed budget, ensure justification aligns with the R&R Budget Form (R.300).
If using a modular budget, justify personnel and consortium costs separately.
search_queries: Justifying personnel effort in NIH grant person months
Modular budget justification requirements NIH
Detailed justification for equipment and supply costs
Narrative justification for consortium/subaward costs
Guidelines for travel justification NIH grant
grant_sections.title: Budget Justification

name: Dissertation Award
